chunk_summary: This section outlines micronutrient and electrolyte repletion protocols for managing malabsorption and persistent diarrhea, emphasizing precise dosing, monitoring, and reassessment to address deficiencies and support recovery.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of micronutrient deficiencies in persistent diarrhea and malabsorption.
Learn specific dosing guidelines for key micronutrients like zinc, vitamin A, and iron.
Recognize the importance of electrolyte repletion and monitoring during recovery.
Develop skills to reassess recovery using hemoglobin, growth velocity, and deficiency panels.
key_facts: Zinc: 10 mg/day for infants <6 months, 20 mg/day for ≥6 months for 10–14 days.
Vitamin A: Single dose of 50,000 IU (<6 months), 100,000 IU (6–12 months), 200,000 IU (>12 months).
Potassium: 3–6 mEq/kg/day in divided doses, with monitoring in dehydrated children.
Iron: 3 mg/kg/day, started only after acute diarrhea resolves and appetite returns.
Vitamin D: Maintenance dose of 200–400 IU/day; higher doses guided by 25(OH)D levels if deficient.
Reassess recovery at 1–3 months using hemoglobin, growth velocity, and deficiency panels.
reference_quote: Micronutrient replacement is core therapy in malabsorption and persistent diarrhea because deficiencies drive anemia, immune dysfunction, and poor linear growth.
document_key_topics: Micronutrient replacement in malabsorption and diarrhea
Dosing guidelines for zinc, vitamin A, and iron
